title: Ink on Cotton
timeEstimate: 15–45 minutes (fresh); aged or heat-set climbs tier
stainChemistry: "Ink" covers multiple chemistries and this card is the catch-all for everything not covered by ink-ballpoint-cotton or ink-permanent-marker-cotton: gel pen, fountain-pen dye ink, rollerball, stamp ink. Most gel and rollerball inks are dye-in-water-plus-glycerin systems; fountain ink is similar with a wetter carrier; stamp ink is typically dye + solvent. Cotton tolerates IPA, POG, 3% H₂O₂, NSD, hot wash. The solvent choice depends on the ink base — water-based inks lift with NSD + cold flush + IPA if needed; solvent-based inks need more aggressive tamp with alcohol or POG. Catch cloth under the stain is the non-negotiable rule regardless of ink type.
whyThisWorks: Catch cloth UNDER + tamp on TOP pattern transfers the dye molecules OUT of the cotton into the cloths instead of smearing sideways. Cold flush first for water-based inks moves the water-soluble part; IPA handles what's left. 3% H₂O₂ clears residual dye color on whites. For solvent-based inks the starting step is the IPA tamp, skipping the cold flush since water does nothing to solvent dyes. Identify the ink class in step 1 — it decides the protocol.
defaultAssumption: Modern gel pens and rollerballs are the most common 'ink on cotton' case outside of ballpoint. Treat as dye-in-water unless the user specifies stamp ink or permanent marker. Caught fresh = high-confidence DIY; heat-set (hot-dried) = often pro.

spottingProtocol.technique: Which ink, then isolate work area
spottingProtocol.instruction: Identify the ink type: water-based (gel pen, fountain, rollerball) → cold flush first works. Solvent-based (stamp ink, Sharpie, permanent marker) → skip water, go straight to IPA tamp. For ballpoint specifically, use ink-ballpoint-cotton. Then place a clean white catch cloth UNDER the stain so the dye transfers down.

For water-based inks: cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Blot. Most fresh gel-pen or fountain ink lightens significantly here. Skip this step entirely for solvent-based ink — water does nothing to it.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Isopropyl alcohol 90%+ on clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply IPA to a clean white cloth. Tamp from the BACK of the fabric, over the catch cloth. Flip the pressing cloth frequently. 3–5 passes on dense ink. Cotton tolerates IPA. Never pour alcohol directly.
Apply NSD, soft brush. Rinse cool. Lifts any residual oil or glycerin vehicle.
For residual dye shadow on whites: 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Oxidizes the color. Skip on dark or dyed cotton. One oxidizer at a time.
Launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — dryer heat sets residual ink permanently.

steps: 1. Identify ink class. Water-based vs solvent-based decides the protocol.
3. Water-based: cold flush first. Solvent-based: skip to IPA.
4. IPA on a white cloth, tamp from back. 3–5 passes.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Catch cloth UNDER + pressing cloth on TOP — non-negotiable
Solvent before water for solvent-based inks — water does nothing and can spread dye
Never pour solvent — tamp with cloth
One oxidizer at a time; skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton

homeSolutions.instruction: Different inks need different treatments. GEL PEN, FOUNTAIN PEN, ROLLERBALL (water-based): cold water does half the work; alcohol finishes. STAMP INK, PERMANENT MARKER, SHARPIE: cold water does nothing — go straight to alcohol. BALLPOINT: see ink-ballpoint-cotton, which has its own protocol. Knowing which type saves you two steps.

homeSolutions.agent: Put a White Towel UNDER the Stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Fold a clean white towel and place it UNDER the stained area. When the ink dissolves, it transfers DOWN into the catch cloth instead of smearing sideways into clean fabric. This is the rule for every ink stain — no exceptions.

For gel pen, fountain pen, or rollerball: hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for 30–60 seconds. A lot of the ink lifts here. Skip this step if the ink is solvent-based (stamp ink, Sharpie).

homeSolutions.agent: Rubbing Alcohol on a White Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab 70%+ rubbing alcohol on a clean white cloth. Press onto the stain from the BACK, over the catch cloth. Flip the pressing cloth frequently. Dye transfers onto whichever cloth is touching it. 3–5 rounds on dense stains.
Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Dawn lifts any residual oil vehicle from the ink. Rinse cool.
For residual blue / purple / red shadow on white cotton: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide, let it bubble 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fumes.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Dryer heat sets residual ink permanently.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate If Heat-Set or Sharpie Plateau
homeSolutions.instruction: If ink has been hot-dried, or if the stain is from Sharpie / permanent marker and doesn't respond to alcohol, take to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Ink on cotton — [type if known], age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with rubbing alcohol + Dawn + 3% H₂O₂. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered]."

steps: 1. Identify ink type — water-based (cold flush helps) vs solvent-based (skip to alcohol).
3. Water-based ink: cold flush from the BACK.
4. Rubbing alcohol on a white cloth, tamp from the back. 3–5 passes.
6. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual shadow (whites only).
7. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
8. Escalate if heat-set or Sharpie plateau.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if ink has been hot-dried, two home rounds failed, or the ink is permanent marker that doesn't respond to alcohol.
warnings: Catch cloth + tamp from back — ink smears sideways without these
Never pour alcohol — tamp with cloth
Water-based vs solvent-based ink need different starts

neverDo: Never skip the catch cloth — sideways dye spread
Never pour alcohol on cotton — tamp with cloth
Never hot-dry residual ink — permanent set
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates IPA, POG, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash
Dyed cotton: IPA usually safe; inside-seam-test if concerned; skip peroxide

materialWarnings: Catch cloth UNDER + tamp on TOP

escalation.when: Ink heat-set by dryer or iron, permanent marker that doesn't respond to alcohol, or two home rounds haven't cleared it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Ink on cotton — [gel / fountain / stamp / permanent marker if known], age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with rubbing alcohol + Dawn + 3% H₂O₂."

contextQuestions: What kind of pen / ink — gel, fountain, rollerball, stamp, Sharpie, ballpoint?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: For ballpoint specifically, ink-ballpoint-cotton has a more targeted protocol. For permanent marker, escalate early — Sharpie pigments rarely clear fully even professionally.
